items.question: How does Manhattan (Minkowski p=1) compare to Euclidean (p=2) for k-NN on the same scaled features?
items.answer: Both sum coordinate-wise gaps, but Euclidean squares each gap before the square root, penalizing a single large difference more aggressively. Manhattan treats each dimension's absolute gap equally in the sum, which can favor axis-aligned structure and be less dominated by one outlier coordinate when p=2 would inflate it.

items.question: When should you prefer cosine similarity over Minkowski/Euclidean distance in production retrieval?
items.answer: Prefer cosine when vector magnitude is not semantically meaningful—typical for text or sentence embeddings where length reflects document size, not content. Prefer Euclidean (usually on scaled or L2-normalized vectors) when both direction and magnitude matter, or when the algorithm assumes L2 geometry (e.g. k-means).

items.question: What if you increase Minkowski p from 1 toward infinity on the same query?
items.answer: Larger p puts increasing weight on the largest coordinate gap relative to smaller gaps; as p grows, the metric approaches Chebyshev (max |uᵢ − vᵢ|) behavior, so neighbors are chosen by worst-feature mismatch rather than total separation across all features.

items.question: What if you L2-normalize all embedding vectors before k-NN with Euclidean distance?
items.answer: All vectors lie on the unit hypersphere; Euclidean distance and cosine similarity become monotonically related for ranking (for unit vectors, smaller L2 distance implies higher cosine). Many teams normalize once and then use either metric interchangeably for neighbor order.

items.question: Cause: one feature column is left unscaled while others are z-scored before Euclidean k-NN. Effect?
items.answer: The unscaled column contributes disproportionately large squared terms in the distance sum, so neighbors are selected primarily by that feature's raw units. Effect on the model: accuracy may look fine on aggregate while predictions ignore subtle signal in properly scaled columns.

items.question: Cause: you switch k-NN from cosine to Euclidean on unnormalized document embeddings. Effect?
items.answer: Longer documents tend to have larger embedding norms, pulling neighbors toward high-norm passages regardless of topic alignment. Effect: retrieval precision drops—irrelevant long documents outrank shorter but more relevant matches.
